target.title: feat(plugins): add guard delivery and subagent review hooks
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the current hook surface can gate tool execution, but it cannot synchronously guard tool results before the agent sees them and it has no explicit pre-spawn / pre-return subagent review phases.
- Why it matters: phase 1-3 guard work needs a typed decision model plus real observation and subagent boundaries, otherwise "auto mode" and third-party policy plugins sit on incomplete seams.
- What changed: added shared guard decision/annotation types, new `before_tool_result_deliver` / `before_subagent_spawn` / `before_subagent_result_deliver` hooks, wired them into tool-result persistence and subagent spawn/announce flows, and extended targeted tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add a guard kernel, model-backed policy runtime, or any bundled automode/firewall plugin yet.

- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/wired-hooks-subagent.test.ts`, `src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.tool-result-persist-hook.test.ts`, `src/agents/sessions-spawn-hooks.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.integration.e2e.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: guard decisions merge consistently, tool results can be warned/blocked before persistence, subagent spawn can be denied before launch, and child results can be rewritten before announce delivery.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests hit the exact hook seams without needing the full policy stack.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing hook wiring tests cover the older lifecycle hooks only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Plugins can now return typed guard decisions and annotations from `before_tool_call`.
- Plugins can synchronously review and rewrite/block tool results before the next model turn via `before_tool_result_deliver`.
- Plugins can synchronously review/block subagent spawn and child-result delivery via `before_subagent_spawn` and `before_subagent_result_deliver`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[tool call] -> [before_tool_call] -> [execute] -> [tool_result_persist] -> [agent sees result]
[subagent spawn] -> [subagent_spawning] -> [child runs] -> [subagent announce]

After:
[tool call] -> [before_tool_call] -> [execute] -> [before_tool_result_deliver] -> [tool_result_persist] -> [agent sees guarded result]
[subagent spawn] -> [before_subagent_spawn] -> [subagent_spawning] -> [child runs] -> [before_subagent_result_deliver] -> [subagent announce]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes

2. Run the corresponding tool/subagent flow.
3. Observe the result warning/block/rewrite before transcript persistence or subagent delivery.

### Actual

target.title: fix(parallel): bound MCP success response body read
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`postMcp` in `parallel-mcp-search.runtime.ts` reads the HTTP response body in a
single ternary expression — but only the **error** branch is bounded:

```ts
text: response.ok
  ? await response.text()                                                   // ← unbounded
  : await readResponseTextLimited(response, PARALLEL_MCP_ERROR_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES),  // ← 8 KiB cap
```

The asymmetry is visible on adjacent lines in the same ternary: `readResponseTextLimited`
is already imported for the error path, yet `response.text()` is used on the
success path with no size guard. A misbehaving or hostile Parallel AI MCP
endpoint can stream an arbitrarily large JSON-RPC body into memory on the
success path, causing OOM — while the identical code one line below already
knows to cap the error body.

This follows the same campaign as Alix-007's `#95103`/`#95218` response-limit
fixes, but applied to the MCP transport layer rather than provider JSON
endpoints.

## Changes

* `extensions/parallel/src/parallel-mcp-search.runtime.ts`:
  - Adds constant `PARALLEL_MCP_SUCCESS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES = 16 * 1024 * 1024`
    (16 MiB — same default as `readProviderJsonResponse`; matches expected
    MCP tool-call payload sizes while guarding against runaway bodies).
  - Replaces `await response.text()` in the success branch of `postMcp` with
    `await readResponseTextLimited(response, PARALLEL_MCP_SUCCESS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES)`.
  - No new imports — `readResponseTextLimited` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http`
    is already present for the error branch in the same expression.
  - No test changes needed — test file already uses real `Response` objects
    and all 17 tests pass unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

* **Behavior addressed:** A Parallel AI MCP endpoint can no longer stream an
  arbitrarily large success body into memory; the success-branch read is now
  capped at 16 MiB, matching the error branch's capped pattern, and the stream
  is cancelled on overflow.
* **Real environment tested:** A real `node:http` `createServer` bound to
  `127.0.0.1`, streaming a 24 MiB body in 64 KiB chunks with **no
  Content-Length** header, driving the real exported `readResponseTextLimited`
  helper on Node v22.22.0.
* **Exact steps:**
  1. Boot server; `GET /huge-success` (200) and `GET /huge-error` (400) stream
     24 MiB each; `GET /small` returns a valid MCP JSON-RPC response.
  2. Drive `readResponseTextLimited(res, 16 MiB)` against `/huge-success` and
     assert text length ≤ 16 MiB and bytes-on-wire stayed near cap.
  3. Drive `readResponseTextLimited(res, 8 KiB)` against `/huge-error` and
     assert text length ≤ 8 KiB and bytes-on-wire stayed near cap.
  4. **Negative control**: run OLD `await response.text()` against
     `/huge-success` and measure total bytes.
  5. Drive against `/small` and assert the JSON-RPC payload is returned intact.
* **Evidence after fix:**
  * Success cap: text length **16,777,216 bytes** (16 MiB); server sent
    **18,677,760 bytes** — near cap, not the full 24 MiB body.
  * Error cap: text length **8,192 bytes** (8 KiB); server sent
    **589,824 bytes** — near cap, not the full 24 MiB body.
  * Negative control: unbounded `response.text()` pulled the **full
    25,165,824 bytes**, proving both caps are load-bearing.
  * Small case: payload returned intact with `"jsonrpc"` field present.
* **Observed result:** `ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED`.
  In-repo Vitest: **1 file, 17 tests — all passed**, including the existing
  `"bounds initialize error bodies without using response.text()"` regression.
* **What was not tested:** Live Parallel AI MCP endpoint calls. The proof
  measures bytes-on-wire and text truncation, which is the load-bearing signal.

## Evidence

```
[case 1] oversized SUCCESS body (200), cap=16 MiB
  ok: bounded at 16 MiB (got 16777216 bytes) — true
  ok: bytes on wire near cap, not full 24 MiB (sent 18677760) — true

[case 2] oversized ERROR body (400), cap=8 KiB
  ok: bounded at 8 KiB (got 8192 bytes) — true
  ok: bytes on wire near cap, not full 24 MiB (sent 589824) — true

[negative control] OLD unbounded response.text()
  ok: unbounded read pulled full ~24 MiB (sent 25165824) — true

[case 3] normal small MCP JSON response
  ok: small response returned intact — true
  ok: content matches original — true

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  17 passed (17)
   Start at  01:09:24
   Duration  1.23s
```

Proof script: `scripts/proof-parallel-mcp-bound.mjs`
Node version: v22.22.0

**Label**: security

AI-assisted.

target.title: fix(codex): expose plugin apps after delayed inventory load
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where native Codex plugin apps could be omitted from a newly started OpenClaw thread when the initial app inventory was missing and plugin activation state changed before thread configuration was built.

## Why This Change Was Made

OpenClaw now remembers when the initial inventory refresh was deferred for plugin activation. If activation does not already require a post-install refresh, it force-refreshes `app/list`, rereads plugin ownership and readiness, and keeps failed activations excluded. The existing install-before-inventory ordering remains unchanged.

## User Impact

Configured, accessible plugin apps remain available when a thread starts during this activation-state transition instead of silently disappearing from that thread's Codex app configuration.

## Evidence

- Added a regression that reproduces the transition from activation-required to already-active while inventory is missing; it failed before the fix and now verifies a forced refresh plus app exposure.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-thread-config.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/plugin-activation.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.binding.test.ts` — 3 files, 65 tests passed.
- Targeted `oxfmt` and `oxlint` checks passed.
- The local staged changed-gate was unable to complete dependency reconciliation because the internal npm mirror returned HTTP 403 without an authorization header; clean PR CI is the authoritative exact-commit gate.

target.body: ## Summary - Fixes incorrect Primary Model selection when switching between agents in Agents > Overview. - Fixes partial / blank Primary Model dropdowns after refresh or first load by ensuring the model catalog is available on Agents Overview paths. - Adds targeted tests for both selection stability and catalog hydration behavior. - Keeps the fix scoped to the Agents Overview model-selection path. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #41344 - Related #46275 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: 1. The agent details subtree could reuse stale DOM/select state when switching agents, which caused the Primary Model select to drift toward the previously viewed agent's state. 2. Agents Overview depended on a shared model catalog that was not guaranteed to be loaded on refresh / first-load / overview-only paths, so the select could render before the catalog was ready and degrade into a partial or blank-looking list. - Missing detection / guardrail: - Existing coverage covered some selected-value behavior, but it did not cover model-catalog hydration timing or the refresh/overview path where the catalog starts empty and arrives later. - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - Prior work around #41344 / #46275 addressed the agent-switching selection issue. Revalidating that fix on latest `upstream/main` exposed a second, related lifecycle issue in Agents Overview model-catalog loading. - Why this regressed now: - The Overview UI depends on model-catalog data, but not all entry paths guaranteed that the catalog was loaded before rendering. - If unknown, what was ruled out: - Ruled out a pure selected-state-only explanation. The partial/blank dropdown after refresh was caused by catalog availability/lifecycle, not just DOM selection drift. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.browser.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: - Switching between agents keeps the correct explicit Primary Model selected. - The inherit-default option text does not go blank. - When the model catalog is initially empty, Agents Overview shows a loading state instead of a misleading partial list. - When the catalog hydrates later, the full list appears without losing the selected value. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - The bug spans both UI render behavior and shared model-catalog lifecycle, so a combination of focused controller/unit tests plus browser-level select behavior tests is the smallest reliable coverage. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - Existing browser coverage covered some selection behavior, but not the catalog hydration path. - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Switching between agents no longer causes the Primary Model select to show the wrong agent's value. - On refresh / first entry to Agents Overview, the Primary Model select no longer degrades into a misleading partial list. - While the model catalog is still loading, the UI now shows a loading state instead of pretending only a small subset of options exists. - Once the catalog loads, the full option list appears without losing the current selection. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Windows 10 - Runtime/container: local dev environment - Model/provider: local UI validation only - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): standard Agents UI configuration with default and non-default agents ### Steps 1. Open Agents > Overview > Model Selection > Primary Model. 2. Switch between a default agent and a non-default agent. 3. Refresh the page and reopen the Primary Model select immediately. 4. Optionally wait for model-catalog hydration or switch away/back to Overview. ### Expected - The select reflects the correct explicit value for the current agent. - The inherit-default option text is stable. - Refresh / first-load does not show a misleading partial catalog. - Once the catalog is available, the full model list appears without losing the selected value. ### Actual - Before this fix, switching agents could retain stale select state from another agent. - Before this fix, refresh / first-load could show only fallback options (for example inherit/current) or appear blank until the catalog became available later. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ### UI Evidence #### Before After refresh / first load, the Primary Model dropdown could show only fallback entries or appear blank. #### After After the fix, the Primary Model dropdown restores the full option list correctly after refresh / first load, without losing the selected value. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Switched repeatedly between default and non-default agents and confirmed Primary Model stays tied to the current agent. - Refreshed the page and reopened Agents Overview to verify the select no longer falls into a misleading partial/blank state. - Confirmed catalog hydration restores the full list without losing the selected value. - Edge cases checked: - Inherit-default text path - Explicit non-default model selection - Refresh / first-load / return-to-overview behavior - What you did **not** verify: - Full end-to-end coverage across unrelated panels/modules - Additional stale/error UX beyond this fix's loading-state behavior ## AI Assistance - This PR was developed with AI assistance (CodeX). - Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: - Multiple UI entry points now ensure model-catalog availability, which could increase repeated refresh attempts if not properly deduped. - Mitigation: - The loading/ensure logic is centralized in the shared model controller and covered by targeted tests. - Risk: - Keyed subtree remounting can discard unsaved local UI state when switching agents. - Mitigation: - That tradeoff is intentional here because correctness of agent-specific model selection is more important than preserving stale subtree state across agent switches.

commands.author: Jerry-Xin

target.title: perf: avoid event-loop stall during embedded_run bootstrap-context
target.body:
## Summary

Fix event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context that block the event loop for 14-22 seconds, causing message loss.

### Root causes addressed

1. **Synchronous file I/O** — `readFileSync`/`closeSync` in workspace file loading blocks the event loop while reading root bootstrap files (AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, etc.)
2. **Unbounded blocking glob walks** — `fs.glob` for extra bootstrap files traverses the entire workspace tree synchronously inside Node, including `.git`/`node_modules` directories, without yielding to the event loop
3. **Uninterrupted hook chains** — `triggerInternalHook` executes all handlers back-to-back without yielding

### Changes

**`src/agents/workspace.ts`**
- Replace sync `readFileSync`/`closeSync` with callback-based async `readFdToString`/`closeFdAsync`
- Parallelize root bootstrap file reads via `Promise.all` (previously serial `for` loop)
- Replace `fs.glob` with an async traversal walker that yields via `setImmediate` every 256 entries, so a sparse pattern like `**/AGENTS.md` over a large workspace never blocks the event loop. The walker performs no directory pruning — it walks the full tree and returns every file matching the configured patterns, with no traversal cutoff and no artificial match cap; the downstream bootstrap character budget owns content limiting.
- Suppress `closeFdAsync` rejections to avoid masking read errors on NFS/FUSE

**`src/hooks/internal-hooks.ts`**
- Add `yieldBetweenHandlers` option with `setImmediate` yield between hook handlers
- Add per-handler timing instrumentation with handler index reporting
- Log warnings for slow handlers exceeding 500ms threshold

**`src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts`**
- Instrument bootstrap-context substages (`workspace-file-load`, `hook-overrides`) with `performance.now` timing
- Log substage durations when total bootstrap-context exceeds 2 seconds
- Enable `yieldBetweenHandlers` for `agent:bootstrap` hook triggers

**`src/agents/bootstrap-files.ts`** / **`src/agents/bootstrap-hooks.ts`**
- Support new async workspace file loading and timing callback interfaces

### Tests

- `workspace.test.ts` — 3 new tests for async fd read (happy path, cache hit, fd close on failure)
- `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` — every matching file returned without an artificial cap (no directory pruning), and a regression test asserting a late-appearing match in a large tree is still loaded (no traversal cutoff)
- `handler.test.ts` — no truncation warning is emitted for large glob scans
- `internal-hooks.test.ts` — 3 new tests for yield-between-handlers ordering, handler timing reporting, and slow handler warning

All existing tests pass without modification.

### Performance impact

- Root bootstrap file loading: serial sync → parallel async (estimated 3-5x speedup for 8 files)
- Glob walks: synchronous/blocking → async with periodic `setImmediate` yields (prevents multi-second event-loop stalls on large workspaces). The walk completes fully; all configured matches are kept, and the existing bootstrap character-budget truncation handles content limiting.
- Hook chains: uninterrupted → yielded (prevents monopolizing the event loop during bootstrap)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Event-loop stalls during `embedded_run` bootstrap-context loading caused by synchronous I/O and a blocking `fs.glob` walk over large workspaces, which blocks the loop and drops messages. This change makes the glob walk async/yielding so the loop stays responsive, while preserving every configured `bootstrap-extra-files` glob match (the earlier iteration of this branch added a hard 20,000-entry traversal cutoff that silently dropped late matches — that cutoff is removed here).
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched OpenClaw checkout on macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`, run via `tsx` against the real exported `loadExtraBootstrapFiles` from `src/agents/workspace.ts`. Before/after captured from two git commits on the PR branch: before = `5849e709f4` (walker with the 20,000-entry cutoff), after = `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed).
- **Real environment scenario**: A workspace of ~26,100 filesystem entries (a 3-tier tree, 30×30×28 filler files) with a single configured-glob target `AGENTS.md` planted in the subtree the depth-first walker drains last, so the target is only reached after more than 20,000 visited entries. A 5ms repeating timer runs during the load as an event-loop liveness probe (steady ticks ⇒ the loop never stalled).
- **Failing proof before fix**: commit `5849e709f4` (cutoff present):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 82.9ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 16 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 0
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: false
  RESULT: late configured glob match was DROPPED (pre-fix behavior)
  ```
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  # against the patched checkout (commit 5ce395d645) a script that builds the
  # ~26,100-entry workspace, starts a 5ms liveness timer, then calls the real
  # loadExtraBootstrapFiles(root, ["**/AGENTS.md"]) and checks the deep match:
  npx tsx proof-live.mts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: terminal output from `node`/`tsx` against commit `5ce395d645` (cutoff removed):
  ```
  approx filesystem entries: ~26100 (old cutoff was 20000)
  elapsed: 99.4ms
  liveness timer ticks during load: 20 (loop responsive)
  matched bootstrap files returned: 1
  deep AGENTS.md (past old 20k cutoff) loaded: true
  RESULT: configured glob match beyond old cutoff is PRESERVED; event loop stayed live
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: With the cutoff removed, the configured `**/AGENTS.md` match located past the old 20,000-entry frontier is now loaded (1 file returned vs 0 before in the terminal output above), so existing broad `bootstrap-extra-files` configs no longer silently lose AGENTS.md/TOOLS.md context. The liveness timer kept ticking throughout the async walk (20 ticks during the ~99ms load), confirming the periodic `setImmediate` yields — not the cutoff — are what keep the event loop responsive.
- **What was not tested**: No full live `embedded_run` with active channel connections was driven end-to-end; this proof exercises the real glob-resolution code path and event-loop liveness directly rather than through a connected channel. The focused regression coverage lives in `workspace.load-extra-bootstrap-files.test.ts` and `handler.test.ts`.

Closes #87509

target.title: fix(ios): stabilize chat streaming layout and session flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: iOS chat streaming could attach to a synthetic placeholder lane, churn during initial history/bootstrap, and reparse streaming content in ways that caused flicker or session drift.
- Why it matters: active conversations can show unstable streaming UI, lose the correct lane/session association, or visibly flicker while tokens arrive.
- What changed: scoped streaming to the active session key, hardened stream acceptance/initial load behavior, and stabilized layout parsing/visibility updates.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway protocol changes, no new model behavior. This fix lives in the shared `OpenClawChatUI` layer used by both iOS and macOS. The bug (cross-run UI drift during concurrent session-key agent events) affects any client using `ChatViewModel`; macOS uses the same chat view and benefits equally, but the fix is not platform-specific — it guards shared streaming state.
- AI-assisted: yes (AI-assisted implementation, contributor-reviewed).
- Testing level: targeted shared-package tests plus iOS build validation.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #40985
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: streaming state was not consistently tied to the active session key, and downstream UI/layout updates re-parsed or re-evaluated visibility in ways that amplified bootstrap churn into visible instability.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was insufficient coverage around session-scoped streaming updates plus layout stability under rapid token updates/history refresh.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): this branch supersedes #40985 by fixing the broader lane/acceptance/layout causes instead of only the earlier visible symptom.
- Why this regressed now: incremental fixes addressed isolated symptoms while the newer streaming lane introduced more state transitions that needed session-scoped and layout-stable handling.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: the issue was not isolated to one text-rendering widget; it involved session scoping, bootstrap timing, and layout parsing together.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/ChatViewModelTests.swift`
  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/AssistantTextParserTests.swift`
- Scenario the test should lock in: session-scoped streaming updates must survive bootstrap/history churn and preserve stable parsed output/visibility while tokens stream.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in shared chat state/parsing logic, so package-level tests cover the core behavior without requiring a full end-to-end harness.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): targeted parser/view-model tests were added/expanded for this branch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- iOS chat streaming stays attached to the active session instead of a synthetic placeholder lane.
- Streaming text/layout behaves more stably during initial load/history refresh.
- Visible flicker/churn from repeated parse/visibility mismatches is reduced.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local dev environment
- Runtime/container: shared OpenClawKit package + iOS app build
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): iOS chat UI / streaming flow
- Relevant config (redacted): local iOS simulator build

### Steps

1. Build the shared package and iOS app from this branch.
2. Open a chat session and trigger a streaming reply.
3. Observe initial history/bootstrap plus active token streaming.

### Expected

- Streaming binds to the active session.
- Bootstrap/history churn does not destabilize the active stream.
- Layout remains visually stable while tokens arrive.

### Actual

- Matched expected in targeted tests and local build validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter ChatViewModelTests`
  - `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter AssistantTextParserTests`
  - `xcodegen generate`
  - `xcodebuild -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClaw -destination 'generic/platform=iOS Simulator' CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO build`
- Edge cases checked:
  - stream acceptance during bootstrap/history load
  - parser stability under repeated streaming updates
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full repository CI matrix
  - non-iOS clients

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
  - All 19 review threads (P1/P2 from `chatgpt-codex-connector[bot]` + `copilot-pull-request-reviewer`) resolved in this update.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A



## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: chat-state timing bugs can reappear under bootstrapping or external-final sync edge cases.
  - Mitigation: this patch scopes state to the active session and adds targeted parser/view-model coverage around the churn paths.



## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: iOS/shared OpenClawChatUI streaming state remains scoped to the active session, load recovery remains idempotent after a failed initial bootstrap plus successful manual refresh, and streaming text/tool state does not get cleared by a later view `load()` call.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS local dev environment, Xcode 26.5 SDKs, Swift Testing 1902, OpenClawKit shared package and generated iOS Xcode project from this PR head `591a724df2531d373fc8603f2faa323fc93bc053`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter ChatViewModelTests
swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter AssistantTextParserTests
xcodegen generate --spec apps/ios/project.yml
xcodebuild -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClawChatUI -destination 'generic/platform=iOS Simulator' CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO build
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
ChatViewModelTests: Test run with 83 tests in 1 suite passed after 5.550 seconds.
AssistantTextParserTests: Test run with 7 tests in 1 suite passed after 0.001 seconds.
xcodegen generate --spec apps/ios/project.yml: Created project at /Users/eulices/projects/openclaw-pr50483/apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj
xcodebuild OpenClawChatUI generic iOS Simulator: ** BUILD SUCCEEDED **
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The recovered-load regression is covered by `successful refresh after failed load makes later load idempotent`, which passed after confirming a failed first bootstrap can recover through manual refresh and a later `load()` does not issue another history request or clear the active stream. The shared `OpenClawChatUI` target also built successfully for iOS Simulator from the generated project.
- **What was not tested**: Full `OpenClaw` app scheme build was attempted but blocked by local Xcode environment missing watchOS 26.5 for the embedded Watch app; the directly changed shared chat UI target was built instead. Full repository CI matrix was left to GitHub.

target.title: fix: drain inbound debounce buffer and followup queues before SIGUSR1 reload
target.body:
## Problem

When `config.patch` triggers a SIGUSR1 restart, two in-memory message buffers are silently wiped, causing inbound messages to be permanently lost:

1. **Inbound debounce buffers** — per-channel debounce timers (`createInboundDebouncer` closure-local `Map` + `setTimeout`) that batch messages over a ~2500ms window. On restart, the `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and buffered messages are garbage collected.

2. **Followup queues** — the `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` global `Map` that collects messages for sessions with active agent turns. Items enqueued but not yet drained are lost when the server reinitializes.

### Reproduction
Send a message on Signal → within 2.5s, trigger a config change (e.g. `config.patch`) → the message is silently dropped.

## Fix

### 1. Global debouncer registry (`src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts`)
- Each `createInboundDebouncer()` now auto-registers in a shared `Symbol.for('openclaw.inboundDebouncers')` global map
- New `flushAll()` method on each debouncer instance that immediately processes all buffered items (clears timeouts, calls `onFlush` callbacks)
- New exported `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` that iterates the registry, flushes all debouncers, and deregisters them

### 2. Followup queue drain waiter (`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts`)
- New `waitForFollowupQueueDrain(timeoutMs)` that polls `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` until all queues finish processing (or timeout)
- Counts `draining: true` queues as having pending work even if `items.length` is momentarily 0

### 3. SIGUSR1 restart integration (`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts`)
Before `markGatewayDraining()`, the restart flow now:
1. `await flushAllInboundDebouncers()` — pushes buffered messages into followup queues
2. `await waitForFollowupQueueDrain(5000)` — gives the drain loops 5s to process newly flushed items
3. Then proceeds with the existing `markGatewayDraining()` → task drain → server close flow

The ordering is critical: flush debouncers first (triggers `onFlush` callbacks that enqueue into followup queues), then wait for followup drain, then mark draining.

## Tests

- **`src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` flushes multiple registered debouncers, returns count, deregisters after flush; `createInboundDebouncer.flushAll` flushes all buffered keys
- **`src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts`**: immediate return when empty, waits for drain completion, returns not-drained on timeout, counts actively draining queues as pending
- **`src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`**: SIGUSR1 restart calls flush before `markGatewayDraining`, skips followup wait when no debouncers had buffered messages, logs warning on followup drain timeout

All 118 tests pass across the 4 affected test files. No type errors introduced.

## Files Changed
- `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts` — global registry + `flushAll` + `flushAllInboundDebouncers()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.ts` — new `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()`
- `src/auto-reply/reply/queue.ts` — barrel export
- `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` — hook into restart flow
- Test files for all of the above

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: When a SIGUSR1 restart fires within the inbound debounce window (~2.5s) or while a followup queue is mid-drain, buffered messages are silently dropped because the in-process `setTimeout` callbacks never fire and the closure-local `Map` is garbage collected. After this fix, `flushAllInboundDebouncers()` immediately flushes every registered debouncer into followup queues and `waitForFollowupQueueDrain()` blocks the SIGUSR1 path until those queues drain or a 5s ceiling is hit.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at HEAD `5d1e6592d02782361b871581c10a6b3c0329105f` of branch `fix/drain-queue-before-sigusr1`. Linux 6.12.67, Node v24.15.0, pnpm-driven monorepo. Patched modules loaded directly with `tsx` from the working tree (no rebuild step), and the built CLI (`pnpm openclaw --version`) reports `OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)` confirming the binary is built from the patched commit.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `pnpm openclaw --version` to confirm the live CLI is built from the patched HEAD.
  2. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303.mts` — small driver that registers two `createInboundDebouncer` instances with a 2500ms debounce, enqueues five items across three keys, then calls `flushAllInboundDebouncers({ timeoutMs: 5000 })` and prints the timing and what each `onFlush` actually received.
  3. `node_modules/.bin/tsx proof-pr46303-drain.mts` — driver that exercises `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` directly against the live `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` `Map` for three cases: empty registry, busy queue that drains at ~120ms, and a queue that stays busy past the timeout.
  4. `pnpm test src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/drain-all.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts` — full targeted suites for all three changed files.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal capture from running the patched modules in this worktree.

  Live CLI build proves the binary contains the patched code:

  ```
  $ pnpm openclaw --version
  OpenClaw 2026.5.4 (5d1e659)
  ```

  `flushAllInboundDebouncers` console output — 5 enqueued items across 2 debouncers and 3 keys, flushed in 1ms instead of waiting 2500ms:

  ```
  [before flush] A delivered=0, B delivered=0 (still buffered)
  [flushAllInboundDebouncers] flushedCount=3 elapsed=1ms (vs 2500ms debounce)
  [after flush] A delivered groups=[["alice:hello","alice:there"],["bob:howdy"]]
  [after flush] B delivered groups=[["ping-1","ping-2"]]
  ```

  `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` console output — three live cases against the real `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` map, including the timeout path that returns `drained:false` with the right `remaining` count:

  ```
  [empty]         {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=0ms
  [busy->drained] {"drained":true,"remaining":0} elapsed=151ms
  [timeout]       {"drained":false,"remaining":2} elapsed=200ms
  ```

  Real test runner output for the changed files (vitest reports the actual function counts; pnpm `test-projects.mjs` shards by project):

  ```
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.cli.config.ts
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  24 passed (24)
     Duration  2.21s
  [test] starting test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply.config.ts
   Test Files  2 passed (2)
        Tests  71 passed (71)
     Duration  4.05s
  [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 11.96s
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: `flushAllInboundDebouncers` deterministically drains buffered Signal-style messages in 1 ms while the natural debounce window is 2500 ms, and reports the right buffer count (`flushedCount=3` for two keys in one debouncer plus one in another). `waitForFollowupQueueDrain` returns immediately when the registry is empty, observes the queue clearing in real time during the busy case (151 ms — one poll interval past the 120 ms cleanup), and correctly returns `drained:false` with `remaining=2` when the timeout fires before drain. The CLI banner reports `(5d1e659)` confirming the built binary contains the patched commit. With the SIGUSR1 hook in `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.ts` calling these helpers in the right order before `markGatewayDraining()`, late inbound messages are pushed into followup queues and given up to 5 s to drain instead of being silently dropped.
- **What was not tested**: An end-to-end real-Signal SIGUSR1 reproduction was not executed on this machine (no signal-cli account paired here); the production restart path is exercised through `src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts`, but a live Signal → `config.patch` → restart roundtrip on real `signal-cli` was not run. Behavior under simultaneous restart-while-flushing on a hung `onFlush` (the registered handle is intentionally retained on flush failure for a future sweep) was not exercised live; that path is covered only by the unit tests in `src/auto-reply/inbound.test.ts`.

target.title: fix(volcengine): bound TTS success response text reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/volcengine/tts.ts` reads its TTS success response bodies with
unbounded `await response.text()` on two paths:

* `seedSpeechTTS` — BytePlus Seed Speech: reads line-delimited JSON frames
  where each frame carries a `data` field with base64-encoded audio.
* `legacyVolcengineTTS` — legacy Volcengine TTS: reads a single JSON body
  carrying the full base64-encoded audio payload.

Both bodies carry base64-encoded PCM/MP3/OGG audio data, which can legitimately
reach several MiB per request. Neither path has any size cap today. A
misbehaving or hostile Volcengine/BytePlus TTS endpoint (including a
self-hosted `baseUrl` override) can stream an arbitrarily large body into memory
before parsing, causing OOM.

This closes the same unbounded-body OOM class targeted by the
`#95103`/`#95108`/`#95218` response-limit campaign, now applied to the
Volcengine TTS success paths.

## Changes

* `extensions/volcengine/tts.ts`:
  - Adds `import { readResponseTextLimited } from "openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http"`.
  - Adds constant `VOLCENGINE_TTS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES = 16 * 1024 * 1024` (16 MiB —
    same default cap used for JSON responses by `readProviderJsonResponse`).
  - Replaces `await response.text()` in `seedSpeechTTS` with
    `readResponseTextLimited(response, VOLCENGINE_TTS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES)`.
  - Replaces `await response.text()` in `legacyVolcengineTTS` with
    `readResponseTextLimited(response, VOLCENGINE_TTS_BODY_LIMIT_BYTES)`.
  - No test file exists for these functions; no test changes needed.

## Real behavior proof

* **Behavior addressed:** A misbehaving Volcengine/BytePlus TTS endpoint can no
  longer stream an arbitrarily large body into memory; both TTS success-body
  reads are now capped at 16 MiB, and the stream is cancelled on overflow.
* **Real environment tested:** A real `node:http` `createServer` bound to
  `127.0.0.1`, streaming a 24 MiB body in 64 KiB chunks with **no
  Content-Length** header, driving the real exported `readResponseTextLimited`
  helper (the same function added to `tts.ts`) on Node v22.22.0.
* **Exact steps:**
  1. Boot server; `GET /huge` streams 24 MiB with no `Content-Length`;
     `GET /small` returns a valid single TTS JSON frame
     (`{"code":0,"data":"<base64>"}`).
  2. Drive `readResponseTextLimited(response, 16 MiB)` against `/huge` and
     assert the returned text length ≤ 16 MiB and bytes-on-wire stayed near cap.
  3. **Negative control**: run the OLD `await response.text()` against `/huge`
     and measure total bytes pushed by server.
  4. Drive against `/small` and assert the frame is returned intact.
* **Evidence after fix:**
  * Bounded case: returned text length **16,777,216 bytes** (exactly 16 MiB cap);
    server sent **17,694,720 bytes** — near the cap, not the full 24 MiB body.
  * Negative control: the unbounded `response.text()` forced the server to push
    the **full 25,165,824 bytes** (~1.5× the bounded read), proving the cap is
    load-bearing.
  * Small case: frame text returned intact with `"code":0` present — no loss.
* **Observed result:** `ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED`. Bounded-reader enforcement
  is covered by the existing `readResponseTextLimited` unit tests in
  `packages/media-core`.
* **What was not tested:** Live Volcengine/BytePlus TTS API calls. The proof
  instead measures bytes-on-wire and text truncation, which is the load-bearing
  signal.

## Evidence

```
[case 1] oversized TTS body, cap=16 MiB, NO content-length
  ok: readResponseTextLimited truncated at cap (got 16777216 bytes) — true
  ok: bytes on wire stayed near cap, not full body (sent 17694720) — true

[negative control] OLD unbounded response.text()
  ok: unbounded read pulled the FULL ~24 MiB body (sent 25165824) — true

[case 3] small valid TTS frame reads intact
  ok: small frame text returned — true
  ok: small frame content intact — true

ALL PROOF ASSERTIONS PASSED
```

Proof script: `scripts/proof-volcengine-tts-bound.mjs`
Node version: v22.22.0

**Label**: security

AI-assisted.
target.author: hugenshen

target.title: fix(chutes-oauth): bound core helper JSON response reads at 16 MiB
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts` had 3 unbounded `await response.json()` calls (userinfo fetch, token exchange, token refresh). A hostile Chutes endpoint, proxy, or CDN can return an oversized HTTP 200 JSON body with no `Content-Length` and stream an arbitrarily large payload — `response.json()` buffers everything into memory before parsing, making this an **OOM vector** on a path that runs with user-attached OAuth credentials.

This is the JSON bounded-read analogue for the deprecated core Chutes helper (the active plugin path at `extensions/chutes/oauth.ts` is covered by sibling PR #96249).

## Changes

- `src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts:119,159,230` — replace 3 `(await response.json()) as Type` calls with `readProviderJsonResponse<Type>(response, "<label>")`. Labels: `"Chutes userinfo"`, `"Chutes token exchange"`, `"Chutes token refresh"`. (+6/-5)

## Real behavior proof

Drives production `readProviderJsonResponse` over a real `node:http` server (127.0.0.1, chunked transfer, no Content-Length). Covers AFTER (capped), BEFORE (unbounded OOM vector), and negative control. Node v22.22.0, Linux x86_64.

```
=== AFTER (16 MiB cap via readProviderJsonResponse) ===

  PASS  hostile oversized body: overflow error thrown
  PASS  valid token exchange response: parsed correctly
  PASS  valid userinfo response: parsed correctly

=== BEFORE (unbounded response.json() — OOM vector) ===

  unbounded read consumed 32.0 MiB — OOM vector confirmed
  PASS  (negative control) unbounded read exceeded 16 MiB cap

=== All chutes-oauth bounded-read assertions: 4/4 passed ===
```

- **Evidence after fix**: 4/4 assertions pass. AFTER: hostile 32 MiB body triggers bounded overflow error, valid token/userinfo responses parse correctly. BEFORE: unbounded read consumes 32.0 MiB (OOM vector confirmed).
- **Behavior addressed**: 3 unbounded `response.json()` calls → capped at 16 MiB via shared SDK helper.
- **Real environment tested**: real `node:http` server (127.0.0.1, chunked transfer, no Content-Length) driving production `readProviderJsonResponse`. Node v22.22.0.
- **Negative control**: unbounded read consumed 32.0 MiB — proves the cap is load-bearing.
- **What was not tested**: live Chutes API call (the proof exercises the same production SDK helper against the same attack shape); cross-platform Node differences.

## Out of scope

- `extensions/chutes/oauth.ts` (active plugin path) — covered by sibling PR #96249.
- Other unbounded `response.json()` call sites in core — covered by bounded-read campaign PRs (#96776, #96772, #96782, etc.).

## Risk

- **Low**: swap to `readProviderJsonResponse` — same error propagation shape, 16 MiB default cap invisible to normal responses (OAuth token responses < 1 KiB). Existing tests: 6/6 pass unchanged.

Sibling PR: #96723 bounds anthropic provider SSE streaming read. #96249 bounds the Chutes OAuth plugin path. Together these close the bounded-read gap for the Anthropic + Chutes OAuth surfaces.

AI-assisted: implemented and proof-tested with AI assistance; reviewed by a human before submission.

target.title: fix(subagents): credit requester-consumed descendant completions
target.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes a lifecycle/delivery accounting gap for background and cron-owned subagent trees: when a requester actually consumes completed descendant subagent output, the descendant run is now durably credited as delivered instead of later being finalized as failed/suspended solely because no separate visible delivery event occurred.

## What Problem This Solves

Requester-owned subagent trees can legitimately consume descendant output without sending a separate descendant-visible delivery event. This is common for cron/background flows where a parent/requester gathers frozen child output, composes its own result, and then delivers that requester result outward. Before this PR, the descendant completion could remain delivery-unproven even though its output had already been consumed by the requester. Later cleanup/finalization could then classify that descendant as failed or suspended, producing delivery state that did not match real behavior. This PR gives the registry an explicit durable signal for that case: requester consumption is delivery evidence only for the exact descendant runs that were consumed, under the matching requester session/run-start window, when the requester path adopts descendant fallback output, so the consumed descendant rows do not later announce or finalize as pending just because the final outward send fails.

## User Impact / Intended Outcome

When OpenClaw can prove that a requester consumed descendant subagent output, the matching descendant run receives durable delivery credit:
- `delivery.status` is set to `"delivered"` for the matched descendant completion;
- requester-consumption metadata is persisted with the run/delivery state;
- cleanup/finalize treats `delivery.status === "delivered"` as completed delivery proof;
- normal visible-delivery paths remain required for completions that were not consumed by a requester.
The matching is intentionally narrow. Credit is only applied when all of these are true:
- the consumed run IDs are explicit;
- the requester session key matches;
- the requester run-start window matches the consuming requester run;
- the consumption kind is known;
- cron fallback credit happens when cron adopts explicit descendant fallback output for the requester result;
- nested subagent announcement credit only covers direct child run IDs that were actually included in the requester-consumed output.

This PR keeps the delivery model explicit:
- if a descendant result was visibly delivered, it is delivered;
- if a requester consumed the descendant output because cron adopted it as the requester result, the descendant is delivered by requester consumption;
- if neither happened, failure/suspension handling still applies.

## Linked Context

- Related issue: #92433 — subagent completion can be silently dropped when an announce handoff reaches a requester transcript but the requester run ends before consuming it. This PR addresses the adjacent durable-accounting problem for completions that *are* consumed by a requester.
- Related issue: #67777 — subagent completion delivery can be lost on direct-announce timeout, drain, or orphan prune. This PR does not implement the full durable inbox/spool requested there, but it narrows one delivery-accounting gap by recording proven requester consumption.
- Related issue: #86537 — suspended final delivery can expire and discard a pending subagent completion. This PR keeps requester-consumed completions from remaining indistinguishable from undelivered completions in finalization state.
- Related issue: #90299 — subagent lifecycle state can report lost active execution context while still surfacing child output. This PR contributes to lifecycle/state consistency when descendant output is actually consumed.
- Related PR: #92934 — required assistant acknowledgement for completion wake handoffs, so transcript commit alone is not treated as requester consumption. This PR complements that by adding durable credit for explicit consumed descendant run IDs.
- Related PR: #35080 — deterministic subagent announce delivery with descendant gating. This PR builds on that direction by making consumed descendant accounting durable.
- Related PR: #44205 — stale descendant cron fallback summaries. This PR keeps the same stale-vs-current discipline for requester-consumed descendant credit.
- Related PR: #95604 — Discord subagent progress feedback. This PR is not a Discord UI change, but accurate delivered/failed lifecycle state is useful to progress/failure-marker consumers.
No exact existing issue/PR was found for `markDescendantCompletionConsumedByRequester` or this exact requester-consumed credit mechanism, so this PR should stand as the focused implementation thread for that behavior.

## What Changes

When OpenClaw can prove that a requester consumed descendant subagent output, the matching descendant run receives durable delivery credit:
- `delivery.status` is set to `"delivered"` for the matched descendant completion;
- requester-consumption metadata is persisted with the run/delivery state;
- cleanup/finalize treats `delivery.status === "delivered"` as completed delivery proof;
- normal visible-delivery paths remain required for completions that were not consumed by a requester.
The matching is intentionally narrow. Credit is only applied when all of these are true:
- the consumed run IDs are explicit;
- the requester session key matches;
- the requester run-start window matches the consuming requester run;
- the consumption kind is known;
- cron fallback credit happens when cron adopts explicit descendant fallback output for the requester result;
- nested subagent announcement credit only covers direct child run IDs that were actually included in the requester-consumed output.

## Implementation Notes

Core registry/runtime changes:
- Adds requester-consumed delivery metadata to subagent registry/delivery state.
- Adds a registry operation for marking descendant completions consumed by requester.
- Persists/restores the new delivery fields.
- Exposes the new runtime operation through the existing registry runtime shims.
Main files:
- `src/agents/subagent-registry-consumption.ts`
  - Implements durable requester-consumption credit.
  - Matches by requester session, explicit run IDs, and run-start window.
  - Records requester-consumption metadata and persists the updated run state.
- `src/agents/subagent-registry.types.ts`
  - Adds delivery/requester-consumption state fields.
- `src/agents/subagent-delivery-state.ts`
  - Persists and restores the new delivery fields.
- `src/agents/subagent-registry.ts`
- `src/agents/subagent-registry-runtime.ts`
- `src/agents/subagent-announce.registry.runtime.ts`
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-subagent-registry.runtime.ts`
  - Wires the new registry operation through runtime boundaries.
Requester-consumption paths:
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/subagent-followup.ts`
  - Reads completed descendant fallback replies and returns both formatted fallback text and the consumed descendant run IDs.
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.ts`
  - Applies cron fallback consumption credit when cron adopts explicit descendant fallback output, before the final outbound send.
- `src/agents/subagent-announce.ts`
  - Credits nested/direct child completions when their output is included in the requester-consumed announcement/wake result.
  - Uses the same latest-child dedupe input for rendering and consumed-run credit so stale child rows are not credited.
- `src/agents/subagent-announce-output.ts`
  - Keeps latest-child dedupe reusable while preserving caller-specific fields such as `runId`.

## Tests / Verification

Coverage added or extended for:
- positive requester-consumed descendant credit;
- no credit for mismatched requester session;
- no credit outside the requester run-start window;
- no credit without explicit consumed run IDs;
- persisted/restored requester-consumption delivery fields;
- cron fallback credit only after successful outbound delivery;
- cleanup/finalize treating `delivery.status === "delivered"` as proof;
- nested/direct child announcement credit;
- stale-vs-current child dedupe, including a negative assertion that stale child run IDs are not credited when stale output is not rendered.
Primary test files:
- `src/agents/subagent-registry-consumption.test.ts`
- `src/agents/subagent-delivery-state.test.ts`
- `src/agents/subagent-registry-completion.test.ts`

## Evidence

### Rebase/Squash/Proof

SQLite persistence now derives `pending_final_delivery` only from active delivery statuses (`pending`, `in_progress`, `suspended`), so delivered/requester-consumed rows that retain payload metadata do not rehydrate as pending.

target.title: fix(copilot-oauth): bound OAuth and API endpoint response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`fetchJson` in `src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.ts` reads the GitHub Copilot
OAuth and API endpoint response bodies with unbounded `await response.text()`
and `await response.json()`. A compromised or hijacked endpoint can stream an
arbitrarily large body and force the runtime to buffer the entire payload — an
OOM/DoS vector.

This is the same vulnerability class already hardened in the Anthropic OAuth
sibling (`src/llm/utils/oauth/anthropic.ts`, PR #96644).

## Changes

- `src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.ts` — import `readResponseWithLimit`
  from `@openclaw/media-core/read-response-with-limit`; replace both
  `await response.text()` and `await response.json()` with
  `readResponseWithLimit` at 16 MiB cap + `new TextDecoder().decode(buffer)` +
  `JSON.parse`. Uses `TextDecoder` to preserve UTF-8 BOM stripping semantics
  that `response.text()` provides.

## Evidence

### Real behavior proof

```text
$ node --import tsx /tmp/verify-copilot-oauth-overflow.mjs
PASS: readResponseWithLimit threw: GitHub Copilot token refresh request response too large: 16777217 bytes
PASS: pullCount = 1 (<= 2 means stream cancelled early)
PASS: stream cancelled = true
```

A ReadableStream enqueues a single 16777217 byte chunk (just over the 16 MiB
cap) into a Response body. `readResponseWithLimit` detects the overflow, throws
via `onOverflow`, and cancels the underlying stream — `pullCount = 1` proves no
further chunks were read, and `cancelled = true` proves the stream was cleaned
up.

### Tests

- **New Vitest case `rejects an oversized Copilot token refresh response`**
  creates a ReadableStream whose first chunk exceeds the 16 MiB cap and proves
  the bounded read cancels the body (`cancel` called once, `pull` count ≤ 2)
  and throws an error containing `"too large"`.
- **Existing 10 Vitest cases unchanged** — device flow, token refresh, model
  listing, timeout, and unsafe lifetime rejection all pass.
- `pnpm test src/llm/utils/oauth/github-copilot.test.ts` → **11 passed (11)**

target.title: fix(agents): enable bundled static catalog fallback for cron Attempt 2
target.body:
## Summary

Plugin-provided models (e.g. opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash) in the bundled static catalog are now resolvable by non-plugin-harness cron sessions via the embedded runner's Attempt 2 path.
- **Problem**: Non-plugin-harness isolated cron sessions fail with "Unknown model" for plugin-provided models, even though the models exist in the bundled static catalog. The embedded runner's two-attempt model resolution in `run.ts:930-984` — Attempt 1 (skipAgentDiscovery) necessarily fails, and Attempt 2 (after `ensureOpenClawModelsJson`) calls `resolveModelAsync` without `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback`, so the static catalog is never queried.
- **Solution**: Pass `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback: true` in the Attempt 2 `resolveModelAsync` call, enabling the static catalog fallback path for plugin-provided models.
- **What changed**: `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts` (+5 lines)
- **What did NOT change**: Attempt 1 behavior (still skipAgentDiscovery); Attempt 1 static catalog fallback for pluginHarnessOwnsTransport; model discovery/registry behavior; configured fallback surface; error handling

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Fixes #95500
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Motivation

The embedded runner's model resolution for non-plugin-harness cron sessions uses a two-attempt strategy (run.ts:930-984). Attempt 1 sets `skipAgentDiscovery=true` (intentionally skips agent discovery for performance). When Attempt 1 fails, `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` runs and Attempt 2 retries without `skipAgentDiscovery`. However, Attempt 2 did not set `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback`, so plugin-provided models registered in the static catalog (like `opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash`) were never queried through the static catalog fallback path. This caused legitimate plugin provider models to fail with "Unknown model" in isolated cron sessions, even though the models were correctly listed in the bundled static catalog.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior addressed**: The embedded runner's Attempt 2 (non-plugin-harness path) now resolves plugin-provided bundled static catalog models via `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback`.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node v22.11.0, branch `fix/static-catalog-fallback-95500` based on origin/main
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```
pnpm vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-embedded-agent.config.ts _proof_95500
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```console
$ pnpm vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-embedded-agent.config.ts _proof_95500 --reporter verbose

 RUN  v4.1.8 /media/vdb/code/github/07 Open Source/02-projects/openclaw

stdout | _proof_95500.test.ts > #95500 root cause verification > Attempt 1: skipAgentDiscovery → fail
  Attempt 1 (skipAgentDiscovery): ❌ Unknown model

stdout | _proof_95500.test.ts > #95500 root cause verification > Attempt 2 without allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback → fail
  Attempt 2 (no fallback):            ❌ Unknown model

stdout | _proof_95500.test.ts > #95500 root cause verification > Attempt 2 with allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback → success
  Attempt 2 (with fallback):           ✅ provider=opencode-go, id=deepseek-v4-flash

 ✓ |agents-embedded-agent| 3 passed
```

> **Note**: The above output is from a real local vitest run on Linux x86_64, Node v22.11.0, branch `fix/static-catalog-fallback-95500` based on origin/main. The proof-only test file was not committed — existing `model.test.ts` already covers `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback: true` in multiple scenarios (lines 664, 717, 821, 940), and a dedicated proof-only test would not add coverage.

**Matrix evidence**: Comparison of three resolution paths for opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash:

| Resolution path | allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Attempt 1: skipAgentDiscovery=true | not set | ❌ Unknown model |
| Attempt 2: full discovery | not set (pre-fix) | ❌ Unknown model |
| Attempt 2: full discovery (this fix) | true | ✅ provider=opencode-go, id=deepseek-v4-flash |

- **Observed result after fix**:
  1. When `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback: true` is set in Attempt 2's `resolveModelAsync` call, plugin-provided models in the bundled static catalog are correctly resolved
  2. Without this flag, Attempt 2 returns "Unknown model" for the same provider/model pair
  3. Attempt 1 behavior is unchanged (intentionally fails for non-plugin-harness)
  4. The fix is additive — no existing behavior is regressed

- **What was not tested**:
  - End-to-end cron session execution with a live plugin provider (requires plugin runtime)
  - Performance impact (single boolean flag addition to an existing options object)
  - Plugin-harness paths (pluginHarnessOwnsTransport already has its own fallback logic)

## Root Cause (if applicable)

The embedded runner's two-attempt model resolution in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts:961-982`:
1. Attempt 1 (line 935-957): `skipAgentDiscovery=true`, `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` only set when `pluginHarnessOwnsTransport=true`
2. If Attempt 1 fails, `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` is called (line 962)
3. Attempt 2 (line 965-982): `resolveModelAsync` called WITHOUT `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback`

The bundled static catalog (`model.static-catalog.ts`) already contains the plugin provider's model entries (e.g., opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash). The only gap is that Attempt 2's `resolveModelAsync` call didn't enable the static catalog fallback path, so the static catalog was never queried.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — The change is additive (a single boolean flag). No existing tests assert that `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` should be false in this code path, so there is no regression risk.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Non-plugin-harness isolated cron sessions configured with a bundled static catalog plugin provider (e.g., opencode-go) will now correctly resolve the model instead of failing with "Unknown model". No other user-visible changes.

## Security Impact (required)

**All checkboxes must be checked or explained**

- [x] New permissions/capabilities? No
- [x] Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- [x] New/changed network calls? No
- [x] Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- [x] Data access scope changed? No

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: All three resolution paths (Attempt 1 without fallback, Attempt 2 without fallback, Attempt 2 with fallback) tested with opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash via focused vitest test
- Edge cases checked: Empty model registry (simulates missing models.json entries); no-op runtime hooks (isolates static catalog resolution from provider-runtime hooks)
- What you did NOT verify: Plugin-harness paths (separate fallback mechanism); configured fallback surface integration

## Compatibility / Migration

- [x] Backward compatible? Yes (additive flag, no existing behavior changes)
- [x] Config/env changes? No
- [x] Migration needed? No

## Best-fix Verdict

- **Best fix**: Yes — The minimal change (5 lines, one boolean flag) corrects a deliberate omission in the Attempt 2 resolution path. The `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` option already existed and was already used by Attempt 1 for the plugin-harness case; this fix simply extends it to Attempt 2 for all harness types.
- **Refactor needed**: No — The two-attempt strategy is well-designed; the fix is a one-flag correction.
- **Alternative considered**: Could have modified `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` to merge plugin catalog entries into `models.json`, but that would be a larger, higher-risk change affecting the model persistence layer. The static catalog already has the correct model data; the issue is only that the resolution path doesn't query it.

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Co-Authored-By**: iCodeMate <noreply@anthropic.com>
- **Human confirmed understanding of code changes**: Yes
- **AI prompts / session excerpts**: Full session containing issue analysis, code path tracing across 6+ files in the cron → embedded-agent-runner model resolution pipeline, root cause verification via vitest proof tests, and implementation

## Risks and Mitigations

**Highest risk area**: If Attempt 2 already had a model resolved via discovery (not the static catalog), the `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` flag is never used — it only activates when `resolveModelAsync` fails its primary resolution paths. No risk of incorrect model selection.

**Mitigations**:
- The flag is only consulted when both discovery and dynamic resolution fail — it's a last-resort fallback
- The static catalog entries are curated and maintained by OpenClaw, not user-provided
- The same flag is already used in Attempt 1 for plugin-harness paths, so the behavior is well-tested

**Compatibility impact**: No breaking changes; existing configurations work without modification.

---

Fixes #95500
